target.title: fix(update): pin post-core plugin compatibility to the downgraded core version (#87914)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** After downgrading OpenClaw (e.g. `2026.5.27` → an older release) while an official plugin such as `@openclaw/diagnostics-prometheus` stays at the newer version, the gateway can flood `log.record` handler errors (the report observed ~98k errors in ~70s) and degrade the event loop. The newer plugin loads against the older core whose API it expects (`isInternalDiagnosticEventMetadata`) is missing.
- **Root cause:** Post-core plugin convergence for a downgrade runs in the current (pre-restart) process, which still reports the old, pre-update `VERSION`. Plugin compatibility (`resolveCompatibilityHostVersion()`) is therefore resolved against the old `VERSION` instead of the freshly installed (downgraded) core, so `updateNpmInstalledPlugins(..., disableOnFailure: true)` does not disable the incompatible newer plugin before restart.
- **Fix:** In the current-process post-core path, when the freshly installed core on disk is older than the running `VERSION` (an actual downgrade), pin `OPENCLAW_COMPATIBILITY_HOST_VERSION` to the installed target for the duration of the plugin convergence, then restore it. Compatibility is then evaluated against the target core, and the incompatible plugin is disabled before the gateway restarts.
- **Intentionally out of scope:** the fresh-process resume path (its `VERSION` already equals the target) and current-process *upgrades* are unchanged. The convergence helper still defaults to `VERSION` when no override is present; that does not affect this scenario because the incompatible plugin is disabled upstream of it.
- **Reviewer focus:** the downgrade gate (`compareSemverStrings(VERSION, installed) > 0`) and the `process.env` set/restore scoping in `updateCommandInternal`.

> Note on triage: the ClawSweeper review suggested mirroring the resume-mode override gated on `downgradeRisk`. In practice `downgradeRisk` is `false` for this scenario — it tracks the requested package target, and a bare version tag or `--no-restart` still reaches this path — so the fix gates on an actual installed-vs-running downgrade instead.

## Linked context

Closes #87914

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** post-rollback `diagnostics-prometheus` `log.record` error storm / degraded gateway event loop.
- **Environment tested:** isolated clone of `openclaw/main`, `pnpm test` via the repo runner.
- **Commands run after this patch:**
  - `pnpm test src/cli/update-cli.test.ts` → **127 passed**, including a new regression test *"pins the compatibility host version to the downgraded target during current-process post-core plugin convergence (#87914)"* — it drives `updateCommand` through an in-process downgrade and asserts `OPENCLAW_COMPATIBILITY_HOST_VERSION` is set to the installed target while `updateNpmInstalledPlugins` runs, then restored afterward. It fails without this patch.
  - `pnpm test src/plugins/install.npm-spec.test.ts src/plugins/update.test.ts src/cli/update-cli/post-core-plugin-convergence.test.ts` → **136 passed** (ClawSweeper's acceptance set).
  - `pnpm tsgo` → **0 errors**; `oxlint` / `oxfmt --check` → clean on the touched files.
- **Observed result after fix:** the in-process downgrade resolves plugin compatibility against the downgraded target, so an incompatible newer plugin is disabled before restart.
- **What was not tested / limitations:** a live macOS package rollback against a real npm registry. The isolated dev clone has no managed-service / rollback environment, and plugin install resolution runs through the gateway. The fix is verified at unit/integration level via the existing update-cli harness, not an end-to-end rollback.
target.author: mukundakatta
